findings.description: ALL external links are broken. GitHub Marketplace link returns 404. Documentation domain (rafayels.engineering/docs) is unreachable (connection refused). Footer 'Issues' and 'Changelog' both point to the same broken marketplace URL instead of specific pages.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: ship a landing page with broken external links — it destroys credibility instantly. INSTEAD: verify all external URLs resolve before deploying; use distinct paths for Issues (/issues) and Changelog (/releases); ensure the docs domain is live or remove the link.

findings.description: Footer links 'Issues' and 'Changelog' are both aliased to the same marketplace URL. Users clicking 'Issues' expect a bug tracker, not a marketplace listing. No differentiation in link targets.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: use identical hrefs for semantically different navigation items. INSTEAD: link Issues to github.com/<owner>/<repo>/issues and Changelog to github.com/<owner>/<repo>/releases.

findings.description: The page is a competent but generic SaaS landing page template. The hero ('One command takes you from idea to merged PR'), pipeline steps, feature cards, and CTA all follow the exact same structure as thousands of developer tool sites. No distinctive visual motif, animation, or interaction pattern sets it apart.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: rely solely on stock landing-page patterns for a developer audience who has seen them hundreds of times. INSTEAD: add a distinctive element — an interactive terminal demo, a live pipeline animation, a code diff visualization, or a unique illustrative style that ties to the engineering identity.

findings.description: Pipeline step cards in the 'The Feature Pipeline' section render as near-empty rounded rectangles at normal viewing. The step numbers, icon, heading, description, and command are there but the visual hierarchy is weak — the colored top borders are the only visual anchor, and the cards feel sparse with excessive whitespace.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: leave cards feeling empty with only a thin color accent. INSTEAD: add more visual weight — larger step numbers, descriptive icons per step, or a connecting visual (timeline line/arrows) between pipeline stages.

findings.description: The light-mode color palette is extremely muted. Body text (#374151) on near-white background (#f8f9fb) is adequate for contrast but the overall page feels washed out and flat. Blue accents are the only color, creating a monochromatic feel.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: depend on a single accent color for the entire page. INSTEAD: use the defined secondary palette (cyan, orange, emerald, violet, rose from CSS vars) more prominently in section dividers, icons, or highlights to create visual rhythm.

findings.description: The 'Engineering' word in the hero uses Instrument Serif italic which is elegant but contrasts oddly with the otherwise utilitarian SaaS aesthetic. The serif display font suggests editorial/luxury but the rest of the page is standard tech product. The brand voice is split.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: mix editorial luxury typography with generic SaaS layouts without bridging the gap visually. INSTEAD: either lean into the editorial quality throughout (more serif usage, editorial spacing, magazine-like layout) or use a display sans that matches the technical tone.

findings.description: The hero CTA 'Install Plugin' scrolls to #get-started which shows terminal commands, but there's no copy-to-clipboard feedback beyond the button itself. The 'See How It Works' link just scrolls to the pipeline section — no demo, video, or interactive walkthrough.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: promise 'how it works' and deliver only static text cards. INSTEAD: add an animated terminal replay, a short video, or an interactive step-through that actually demonstrates the workflow.

findings.description: The capability cards (Multi-Agent Review, ADRs, Git Worktrees, Knowledge Compounding, Browser Testing, Dev Logs) use small emoji-like icons that appear as tiny colored dots at normal viewport size. The icons don't communicate the feature's purpose at a glance.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: use tiny abstract icons that are indistinguishable at reading distance. INSTEAD: use larger, more descriptive SVG icons or illustrations that immediately convey each capability.

findings.description: The sticky navigation only appears after scrolling (transform: translateY(-100%) by default, becomes visible via JS). Users landing on the page have no persistent nav until they scroll. The top-of-page has no visible navigation bar.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: hide the primary navigation on initial page load. INSTEAD: show the nav immediately (perhaps with a transparent background) so users can orient themselves, then transition to the sticky compact style on scroll.

findings.description: The agent cards grid in '29 Specialized Agents' section only shows 12 agents with a '+17 more' link. The grid is dense with small text and the agent type badges (Review, Research, Workflow, Design) use minimal color differentiation.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: cram a data grid into a marketing page without visual breathing room. INSTEAD: group agents by type with clear section headers and color-coded backgrounds, or use a compact interactive expandable list.

findings.description: No dark/light mode toggle despite having both themes defined in CSS. Users in dark-mode OS get the dark theme automatically but cannot override it. No user control over appearance.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: implement dual themes via prefers-color-scheme alone with no manual toggle. INSTEAD: add a theme toggle in the nav that persists preference to localStorage.

findings.description: Good: focus-visible styles are defined with cyan outlines and box shadows. Good: skip-link is present. Good: semantic HTML with aria-labels on sections. Good: lang='en' is set. These are above-average accessibility fundamentals.
findings.recommendation: No change needed — maintain these accessibility patterns.

summary: A competent, well-structured developer tool landing page with strong technical foundations (semantic HTML, accessibility landmarks, skip links, focus-visible styles, responsive design, smooth hover transitions, light/dark theme support). However, it is critically undermined by ALL external links being broken (GitHub Marketplace 404, docs domain unreachable, deduplicated footer URLs), which destroys user trust and renders the page non-functional as a conversion tool. Visually it is a generic SaaS template — clean but undifferentiated — with a muted monochromatic palette, sparse pipeline cards, and tiny indistinguishable feature icons. It needs broken links fixed immediately (P0), stronger visual identity, and at least one distinctive interactive element to stand out.
